caveats: a: The occupational source is four-digit NAICS 326100 rather than six-digit 326160.
a: AI task exposure is inferred rather than measured.
a: Existing machine and vision automation is excluded conceptually but not observable in the source table.
rho: The cited occupation group includes metalworking and processes unlike bottle manufacturing.
rho: Implementation depends heavily on plant age, SKU changeover frequency, and customer validation requirements.
e: The frozen firm-count estimate is margin-bridged rather than observed.
e: NAICS establishment classification does not disclose independent ownership or recurring-customer status.
s5: Cross-industry survey intentions are not completed transactions.
s5: No six-digit owner-age or deal-flow series was found.
q: No public contract sample quantifies pass-through for independent bottle manufacturers.
q: Retention varies sharply with custom tooling, qualification costs, and customer concentration.
d5: The range is judgment rather than a constant-price six-digit forecast.
d5: End-market and resin mix differ materially across bottle plants.
o: Operator requirement is inferred from the physical manufacturing activity.
o: Packaging substitution is also reflected in d5, so o is limited to insourcing, self-service, and software-only displacement.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Predominantly repeat B2B sales under customer specifications, purchase orders, supply agreements, and negotiated unit pricing; revenue is volume multiplied by per-unit bottle prices, often with resin-cost adjustment mechanisms.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house bottle plants, plastic containers other than bottles, resin production, distributors without manufacturing operations, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers producing plastic bottles for external food, beverage, household, personal-care, pharmaceutical, and industrial customers through recurring or repeat supply relationships.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for the broader NAICS 326100 plastics-product industry, not task-level AI exposure in 326160. I map clerical, sales, planning, quality-documentation, and a limited portion of production-monitoring work into an exposed wage share; inclusion of other plastics products may over- or understate bottle-plant automation depending on plant scale and process mix.
caveats: The occupational source is four-digit NAICS 326100 rather than six-digit 326160.
AI task exposure is inferred rather than measured.
Existing machine and vision automation is excluded conceptually but not observable in the source table.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from 8-12 independent bottle plants, split by SOC and current automation status, plus time-and-motion data for quoting, scheduling, QA documentation, inspection, and maintenance diagnosis.
a.rationale: Bottle plants remain production-heavy, so the principal AI opportunity is in production planning, quoting, procurement, quality records, maintenance triage, vision-assisted inspection, and back office rather than wholesale replacement of machine operators. The broader-industry OEWS table shows a substantial production workforce and therefore constrains the exposed share below generic office-sector estimates.

caveats: The range is judgment rather than a constant-price six-digit forecast.
End-market and resin mix differ materially across bottle plants.
d5.change_evidence: Customer-by-end-market five-year unit forecasts, industry shipment volumes separated from price/mix, resin-converter capacity additions, and signed packaging-conversion programs at major bottle buyers.
d5.rationale: Bottles serve recurring consumer and industrial end markets, supporting broadly stable physical demand. Lightweighting, refill formats, recycled-content rules, substitution to aluminum, glass, paper, and flexible packaging, and offshore competition cap growth and can drive contraction, while reshoring and population-linked consumption support the upper bound.

caveats: The frozen firm-count estimate is margin-bridged rather than observed.
NAICS establishment classification does not disclose independent ownership or recurring-customer status.
e.change_evidence: A complete establishment-to-owner map using Data Axle or D&B, combined with customer concentration, captive status, plant ownership, and normalized EBITDA from broker books or confidential financials.
e.rationale: Most establishments in the code make tangible bottles for outside customers and repeat supply is common, but the eligible share of LMM firms is reduced by captive plants, single-customer dependence, obsolete equipment, environmental liabilities, and operations whose normalized EBITDA or control economics do not fit the lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 326160 ($2,726,743,329) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 326160 ($17,107,679,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $453,268 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 326160); class <5: 30 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,436,100 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $210,389 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,067,615 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $302,906 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,989,706 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $584,492 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 67 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,321,567 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,244,610 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 35 firms, avg revenue/firm $55,706,286 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,160,971 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $636,073,955 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $93,184,834 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 102

caveats: Operator requirement is inferred from the physical manufacturing activity.
Packaging substitution is also reflected in d5, so o is limited to insourcing, self-service, and software-only displacement.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy plans, announcements of captive bottle-line investment, contract-manufacturing renewal data, and evidence of commercial fully autonomous plants without an accountable operator.
o.rationale: Customers will still need an accountable physical manufacturer to convert resin, control tooling and processes, certify quality, and deliver bottles. Software can improve operations but cannot independently supply the physical current-service basket; the main displacement risk is customer insourcing or packaging substitution.

caveats: No public contract sample quantifies pass-through for independent bottle manufacturers.
Retention varies sharply with custom tooling, qualification costs, and customer concentration.
q.change_evidence: A sample of 20 customer contracts and renewal histories showing indexation, open-book clauses, term length, rebid cadence, realized price-downs, and contribution margins before and after automation projects.
q.rationale: Manufacturers can retain benefits during contract periods and through better uptime, scrap, and service, but sophisticated packaging customers rebid supply, negotiate open-book resin adjustments, and capture part of visible cost reductions. Unit-price competition limits permanent retention.

rho.bridge: BLS describes economy-wide metal and plastic machine-worker technology adoption and employment projections, not five-year implementation in bottle manufacturers. I translate evidence that manufacturers are expanding CNC, robotics, and other labor-saving machinery into a moderate implementation rate; capital constraints, validation, uptime risk, and integration make the proxy more likely to overstate adoption for small plants.
caveats: The cited occupation group includes metalworking and processes unlike bottle manufacturing.
Implementation depends heavily on plant age, SKU changeover frequency, and customer validation requirements.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level capex plans, MES/ERP and vision-system adoption rates, completed automation-project paybacks, and interviews with controls integrators serving independent blow molders.
rho.rationale: Digital back-office and planning tools can be deployed within five years, while line-level changes require integration with blow-molding equipment, sensors, quality systems, and customer qualification. The result is meaningful but incomplete implementation of the exposed opportunity.

s5.bridge: Gallup surveys US employer-business owners across industries about five-year plans, not eligible plastics-bottle firms or completed control transfers. I discount stated sell/transfer intentions for non-completion, family or employee transfers that may not be purchasable, and plant closures; the general-owner proxy may overstate qualifying external control transfers.
caveats: Cross-industry survey intentions are not completed transactions.
No six-digit owner-age or deal-flow series was found.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed transactions from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, and plastics-industry brokers matched to the eligible-firm denominator, plus owner-age and succession-plan interviews.
s5.rationale: Owner succession creates a credible transfer pipeline, but planned transitions do not all close and smaller manufacturers can instead liquidate equipment, sell to a strategic buyer outside the lens, or transfer internally.

principal_driver: Repeat physical demand combined with improvable planning, quality, uptime, and administrative workflows.
principal_weakness: Capital intensity and buyer bargaining power constrain implementation and long-run retention of savings.

sources.supports: Confirms that the source covers employers of all sizes in NAICS 326100 and provides its occupation employment and wage mix, including a large production-occupation presence used to bound AI task exposure.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 326100 Plastics Product Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports a projected 7 percent employment decline from 2024 to 2034 and explains that firms continue expanding CNC tools, robots, and other labor-saving machinery while computerized equipment still requires skilled operation and maintenance.
sources.title: Metal and Plastic Machine Workers, Occupational Outlook Handbook

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments primarily engaged in manufacturing plastics bottles and distinguishes other plastic containers, grounding the lens and physical-operator requirement.
sources.title: 2007 NAICS Definition: 326160 Plastics Bottle Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports a fall 2024 survey of employer-business owners about plans over the next five years and that 74 percent of owners planning for retirement intend to sell or transfer ownership, used only as a cross-industry transfer proxy.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

title: Plastics Bottle Manufacturing
